We will see in this unit our third computational model, the WHILE language. The WHILE language can be seen as a simplified programming language, as it only uses natural numbers, and has only four different assignment statements and a single iterative instruction. This model will permit us to analyze features like the function computed by a program and its the temporal complexity. UNIT 10 The WHILE language 10.1 Introduction AJ Kfoury, MA Arbib, RN Moll, A programming approach to computability, Springer Verlag, 1982 The four assignment statements for setting the values of variables are: -- Assign a variable the zero constant value : X i := 0 -- Assign a variable the value of other variable: X i := X j -- Assign a variable the value of other variable incremented by one unit: X i := X j + 1 -- Assign a variable the value of other variable decremented by one unit: X i := X j – 1 In a program written in the language WHILE (i.e., a WHILE program) the variable identifiers have the form: X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X i , ... The iterative control instruction is an indefinite loop (a “while” instruction), that has also a very simple control condition: checking whether the value of a given variable (the control variable) is not equal to zero. The control instruction is an indefinite loop of the form : while X i  0 do (the header of the loop) code (the body of the loop) od (the tail or end of the loop) A code is a finite non-empty sequence of statements separated by the symbol “;”. Note: The iterative control sentence is refereed as "loop", "while loop" or simply "while" sentence. We say that a program is something more than a code as a WHILE program is a triple (n, p, s), where n and p are naturals with p  n and p > 0 ; and s is a code following the previous sintaxis. If a WHILE program is a triple (n, p, s) then the code has p variables in use: ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X p ) that can take natural values. n out of the p variables are input variables ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X n ), and the rest are denoted auxiliar variables. The (p – n) auxiliar variables are implicitly set to zero at the beggining of the execution of the program. There is no input-output instructions, as the output of the program is the value of variable X 1 when the code finishes. The functioning of a WHILE program consists (as it is a model based on a structural language) in executing sequencially the instructions that compose the code, starting from the first one. The rest of the statements are executed once at a time when the previous one has fnished. The decremental

The functioning of a WHILE program consists (as it is a model based on a structural language) in executing sequencially the instructions that compose the code, starting from the first one. The rest of the statements are executed once at a time when the previous one has fnished. The decremental assignment statement ( X i := X j – 1 ) is executed in a way such that the value of the variable is zero in case that the decremental variable value was zero before the execution. In relationship to the iterative control instruction (while sentence), this first check whether the control variable (the one in the header) is different from zero; and in this case executes the code in the body of the loop, checking again the value of the control variable after this. This procedure is repeated while the checked variable is not equal to zero, as in one of these checks the value of the control variable is zero the while loop finishes. We can associate a single function from N n to N to each WHILE program. This function, that we will defined later as the function computed by a WHILE program, can be a partial function, as it could happen that the program will never finish for certain inputs. In order to formally defined this computational model based on the While language we will use some concepts related to the theory of formal languages that we saw in previous units. The header of a loop does not alter the content of the variables and after its execution the program jumps to the next line if the control variable is different from 0; or to the next line after the end of the loop if the control variable is equal to zero. The Equivalence theorem says that the different formal models, proposed for the concept of algorithm and computable func;ons, are all equivalent. In order to proof this statement we will need to add some other models to the three ones we have seen so far, and perform a circular demonstraEon of mutual inclusion between them. Program Simul, that simulates the functioning of program z with memory m (gödelization of the input vector x ), is presented next. Definition 12.14: Program Simul Simul(z, m) z := z+1 ; (* add one as we took 1 when codifying *) while l(z)  0 do (* while there are pending atatements *) s := degod(z,1) ; (* the number of the first statement *) if type(s)  3 then (* if its is an assignment *) m := Execute(s, m) ; (* execute it: update the value of the variable modified *) z := Reduce(z) ; (* eliminate the

pending atatements *) s := degod(z,1) ; (* the number of the first statement *) if type(s)  3 then (* if its is an assignment *) m := Execute(s, m) ; (* execute it: update the value of the variable modified *) z := Reduce(z) ; (* eliminate the statement from z *) else (* if is a while loop *) if degod(m, extr(s,1))  0 do (* if the control variable is not equal to 0 *) z := Add2loop (z, extr(s,2)) (* add loop code numbers to z*) else (* if the control variable is zero *) z := Reduce(z) (* eliminate the statement *) fi fi od ; X 1 := m The output of Simul is a number that codifies the value of all variables Program Simul uses the programs Execute, Reduce and Add2loop Before seeing the programs Execute, Reduce and Add2loop, we need to introduce the function replace. Definition 12.15: Function replace replace: N 3 → N replace(z,k,x) = god(degod(z,1), ..., degod(z,l(z)), 0, ..., 0, x)ifk > l(z) l(z) > 0 god(0, ..., 0, x)if k > l(z) l(z) = 0 z if k = 0 god(degod(z,1), ..., degod(z,k-1), x, degod(z,k+1), ...,degod(z,l(z))) if k l(z) k 0 Note: replace (z,k,x) is the code of the vector obtained after replacing in the vector codified by z, the component k for the value of x . Definition 12.16: Program Execute (s, m) if type (s) = 0 then m:= replace (m, extr(s,1), 0) fi; if type(s) = 1 then m:= replace (m, extr(s,1), degod(m, extr(s,2))) fi; if type(s) = 2 then m:= replace (m, extr(s,1), degod(m, extr(s,2))+1) fi; if type(s) = 3 then m:= replace (m, extr(s,1), degod(m, extr(s,2))-1) fi; X 1 := m Let ́s now introduce the programa Execute that executes the assignments; the program Reduce that eliminates the first pending instruction from the top of z ; and the program Add2loop that adds to the top of z the sequence of instructions (its codes) of the body of a loop. Definition 12.17: Program Reduce(z) Reduce(z) if l(z) < 2 thenz:= 0 elsez:= god(degod(z,2), degod(z,3), ..., degod(z,l(z))) fi; X 1 := z Definition 12.18: Program add2loop Add2loop (z,s) X 1 := god(degod(s,1), ..., degod(s,l(s)), degod(z,1), ..., degod(z,l(z))) Note: What is added to the top of z is not the code of the body of the loop but the sequence of instructions codified by s. This is why the degod function is applied to s before applying the function god to z.

UNIT 13 Limits of computa-on -­‐-­‐ The Hal.ng problem -­‐-­‐ The busy beaver func.on 1 13.1 The Hal-ng problem Let's see first what a problem associated to a predicate is. Given a predicate P of n arguments, and an input vector a ∈ N n , problem P associated to the given predicate (both are denoted P), consists in the following ques-on: Is predicate P true for input vector a ? This ques.on is equivalent to: Is P(a) True ? Or just P(a) ? Note: Any input Vector a is an instance of problem P. 2 As problems are directly associated to predicates, the classifica.on of them is associated to the classifica.on of the predicates. The following table shows the classifica.on of the problems according to the classifica.on of Predicates and associated func.ons. 3 Defini-on 13.1: Predicate H Let g be the code number of a WHILE program. Let x be an input vector for program g . H(g, x ) is True iff program g for input x finishes. Defini-on 13.2: Predicate H n Let g be the code number of a program WHILE of n inputs. Let x be an input vector for program g ( x ∈ N n ). H n (g, x ) is True iff program g for input x finishes. The Hal*ng problem is the problem associated to predicate H (note that the same nota.on is used for both the problem and the predicate). The Hal-ng problem for a program with n inputs is the problem associated to predicate H n (same nota.on in both cases). 4 Theorem 13.3: H 1 is not solvable. Proof: We will demonstrate the theorem by contradic.on. Let's assume that: Problem H 1 is solvable ⇓ Predicate H 1 is decidable ⇓ ∃ H 1 ∈ T-­‐REC | H 1 is the characteris.c func.on of predicate H 1 That is saying that Func.on H 1 , defined below, belongs to T-­‐REC : H 1 (g, x) = 1 if the program codified by g halts on input x 0 if the program codified by g does not halt on input x 5 Let's consider the func.on bad , defined as: bad(x) = 1 if H 1 (x,x) = 0 ↑ if H 1 (x,x) = 1 The func.on bad can be computed using the following macroprogram WHILE bad = (1,2,c) with c : X 2 := X 2 + 1 ; While H 1 ( X 1 , X 1 ) ≠ 0 do X 2 := 0 od ; X 1 := X 2 6 Program bad is a WHILE program and then has a code number. Let m be this number. What is the value of H 1 (m, m) ? • If H 1 (m, m) = 1 ⇒ program m with input m Halts ⇒ bad(m) = ↑ ↑ ↑ by defini.on of H 1 by defini.on of func.on bad but then ⇒ H 1 (m, m) = 0  arriving to a contradicHon

H 1 (m, m) ? • If H 1 (m, m) = 1 ⇒ program m with input m Halts ⇒ bad(m) = ↑ ↑ ↑ by defini.on of H 1 by defini.on of func.on bad but then ⇒ H 1 (m, m) = 0  arriving to a contradicHon ↑ because m is the code for bad and does not halt (↑) , then according to the definiHon of H 1 7 8 • If H 1 (m, m) = 0 ⇒ program m with input m does not halt ⇒ bad(m) = 1 ↑ ↑ by defini.on of H 1 by defini.on of bad ⇒ H 1 (m, m) = 1  ↑ because m is the code for bad, and if bad finishes, according to the defini.on of H 1. In both cases the reasoning leads to a contradic.on, and then the assump.on we made that H 1 is solvable is not a valid assump-on and thus: H 1 is not solvable Note: This result can be extended to H n and to H . Let’s consider the other possibility: Proposi-on 13.4: H 1 is par.ally solvable. Proof: Given the func.on f : N 2 → N defined as: f = σ ( U[REC 1 ] ) we see that : > 0 iff program g with input x finishes. f(g, x) ↑ iff program g with input x does not finish then P f = H 1 ⇒ H 1 ∈ PRED(REC) ⇒ H 1 is par.ally solvable ↑ f ∈ REC Predicate H 1 is parHally decidable and funcHon H 1 is parHally solvable Note:This result can also be extended to H n and to H . 9 13.2 Busy Beaver func-on ("Castor afanoso") We will see now an example of non-­‐computable func.on; i.e., a func.on that is not WHILE-­‐computable, nor Turing-­‐computable, neither μ-­‐recursive. But, first we will define the length of a program and the set P n . Defini-on 13.5: Length of a program WHILE (length), P n Given Q ∈ WHILE, with Q = (n, p, s) . The length of a program WHILE is defined as: length: WHILE → N length(Q) = |s| do + |s| := The set of all programs WHILE of length n will be named P n , that is: P n = { Q ∈ WHILE | length(Q) = n } Note: The length of a program is the number of statements (assignment statements and While loops), while the size of a program (cf. defini.ons 10.3 & 10.4) is the number of lines of the code. 10 Example 13.6: Given Q = (1, 2, s) with s as follows: X 2 := X 2 + 1 ; While X 1 ≠ 0 do X 2 := 0 od ; X 1 := X 2 We have that: Q ∈ P 4 as length(Q) = 4 (size(Q)=5). Example 2.29:Given the following language: L 1 = { w {a, b} * | wdoes not contain the substring bbb } . A recognizing device of L 1 could be the following: We keep reading the symbols of the input string from left to right and (after initialization of a counter to zero). 1º. A counter is resetted (put to zero) every time we read an ‘a’. 2º.Add one to the counter every time a ‘b’ is encountered. 3º.Stop and answer “NO” if the counter reach three. 4º.Stop and answer “YES” if the string is read completely and the counter has not reached three. A generating device of L 1 could be the following: “ For producing a member of L 1 first we write nothing or we write ‘b’ or ‘bb’; then we repeat, zero or more times the operation of writing ‘a’, or ‘ab’ or ‘abb’ ”. Contrary to the recognizing devices, the generating devices are not algorithms, as they can be ambiguous (free choice of options). UNIT 4 Finite Automata We will see in this unit a restricted model of computation called a Finite Automaton, to see later on more sophisticated devices for accepting and generating languages. Finite Automata (FA) are no other than a different way of representing regular languages, in particular they are regular language recognition devices. FA shares with a computer the fact that they have “central processing unit” of fixed, finite capacity. It receives its input as a string and delivered no output except an indication of whether or not the input is considered acceptable. What makes a FA a restricted system is the absence of memory outside its fixed central processor. We will see first, deterministic FA, and then the non-deterministic ones, to see later an equivalence between them. 1 2 4.1 Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) A deterministic finite automaton (DFA) is a mathematical model of a discrete system comprising a finite set of possible internal states, and where the actual internal state of the system is the only information about previous inputs. Then, the behaviour of a DFA will be determined, exclusively, by its actual state and the future inputs. Let ́s see before given a formal definition of a DFA , its structure and functioning. A DFA is a language recognizing device, in particular, of regular languages. The input string to be (or not) recognized is given by an input tape, divided in blocks containing a symbol in each block. Think a DFA as a ideal machine, according to the following description: The finite control is a "black box" that can be in a single state from a finite set of possible ones. Each state is labelled as final or not final, and there is always one state corresponding to the initial state (usually q 0 ) in which the finite control is at the begining. The reading head is controlled by the finite control and send to it the content of the reading block. The head can only move one block to the right and starts reading from the left of the input tape. The finite control, depending of the read symbol and of the present state, changes its state and moves the reading head. When the end of the tape is reached, the DFA stops. If the automaton finishes in a state belonging to the set of “final states” then the string is accepted. The accepted language by the DFA is the set of accepted strings. 3 Definition 4.1: Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) A deterministic finite automata (DFA) is a quintuple M = ( K ,  ,  , s , F ) where: K is a finite non empty set of states;  is an alphabet; s is the initial state (s  K ) ; F is the set of final states (F  K ) ;  is a transition function defined as : K   → K . The introduction of non-determinism gives new possibilities for the automata, for example for a given input and state, several next states can be possible (even none). Thus for a given configuration there are several (even none) next configurations. Also it is possible to read more than one symbol in a single step, and evenmore to transit between states reading the empty string. Non-determinism permits that for a single string there will be several computations, and that for some of them the automata can be blocked (there is no next possible configurations even if there is still symbols of the string to be read). Blocked computation are considered non accepted computations. A string is accepted by a NDFA if presenting the string as input there is a computation that accepts it. Unfortunately, NDFA accepts the same languages as the DFA, the regular ones. However, their introduction is useful, as a NDFA has less number of states and transitions than a DFA that accepts the same language. 15 Definition 4.21: A Non Deterministic Finite Automata (NDFA) is a quintuple M = ( K ,  ,  , s , F ) where: K is a non-empty finite set of states;  is an alphabet; s is the initial state (s  K ); F is the set of final states (F  K );  is a relation defined as a finite subset of K   *  K . PART II -- Theory of Computation Unit 8: The Turing Machine 8.1 Introduction 8.2 Formal definition 8.3 Examples The Turing Machine Introduced in 1936 by the English mathematician Alan Turing, the Turing machine is an ideal machine that seems capable of computing every function that can be computed (Church-Turing Thesis). Any efforts to augment its computing capabilities have failed and then we can see the Turing machines as a stable and maximal class of computational devices. In essence , a Turing Machine (TM) consists of a finite-state control unit and an infinite tape. TM • • •• • • The tape is unidimensional and left and right directions are clearly defined. Every cell contains a symbol of the TM alphabet or a special symbol (*) to indicate that the cell is empty. The TM has a single Read/Write head, that in a given moment see only one cell, the scanned cell of the tape. infinite tape Finite-state control head The number of occupied cells on the tape is finite, and the whole content of the tape is denoted as tape expression. The control unit operates in discrete steps; at each step it performs an elemental operation according its current state and the tape symbol currently scanned by the head. A TM can execute one of the following elemental operations: • Write a symbol of the alphabet on the actual cell. • Move the head one cell to the right. • Move the head one cell to the left. • Halt (stop). It also decides what the following state of the control unit will be. At any given time a TM is in one of several possible states, except at the beginning of the operation when the TM is in the initial state. Thus, before a computing step, that always occurrs except when the elemental instruction is to halt, the whole information material is: - the tape expression - the current scanned cell and on the TM: - current state. After the computing step we have: - a new tape expression (it can be the same one of the previous step) - a new scanned cell (it can be the same of the previous step) and, on the TM: a new current state (it can be the same of the previous step). A Turing Machine (TM) is a quintuple M = ( K , q 0 , Σ , γ , δ ) where: K is a non-empty finite set of states ; q 0 is the initial state ( q 0 ∈ K ); Σ is an alphabet (a non-empty finite set of symbols); γ is an instruction function defined as: γ : K × Σ ∪ { a 0 } → Σ ́ ; δ is a transition function defined as δ : K × Σ ∪ { a 0 } → K . Definition 8.1: Turing Machine (TM) Alphabet Σ = { a 1 , a 2 , a 3 , ... , a n } with n ≥ 1 and with r, l, h ∉ Σ . a 0 represents the empty symbol, with a 0 ∉ Σ (usually we will use a 0 = * ) Let’s define Σ ́ = Σ ∪ { a 0 , r , l , h } . We will represent a TM M = ( K , q 0 ,  ,  ,  ) as a 4 columns matrix with ||K||(n+1) rows, corresponding to the values of  (instruction function) and  (transition function) for all combinations of states ( q j ) and scanned symbols (a i ): Usually if ( q j , a i ) = h then ( q j , a i ) = q j , but a different value will not modify the functioning of the TM. Definition 8.2: A tape expression of a TM M = ( K , q 0 ,  ,  ,  ) is any application E : Z →   { a 0 } that verifies || { z  Z | E(z)  a 0 } ||  N . The application E assigns to every cell a symbol of the alphabet or the empty symbol, with the condition that only a finite set of the cells is not empty. This condition ensures that the application E is always representable. Cell z is the one located immediate to the left from cell z+1, in a way that the tape is countable and infinite to the right and to the left, and E(z) is the content of cell z . • • •• • • z z+1 Definition 8.3: A configuration of a TM M = ( K , q 0 ,  ,  ,  ) is any triple (set of three elements) (q, E, z) where: q  K ; E is a tape expression of M ; z  Z . Note: A configuration represents the actual state (q) of the TM, the complete content of the tape (E), and the position of the head on the tape (z). Definition 8.4: Given a TM, M = ( K , q 0 ,  ,  ,  ), an initial configuration is any configuration of the form (q 0 , E, z) . Note: The question whether a given TM will halt or not starting form an initial configuration is not easy to answer for the general case. In fact, this question is known as the “Halting problem” that we will analyzed in future units. When a TM is designed, the computations of interest are the well initiated ones. It is possible to show that all standard arithmetic operations, and also those that can be obtained from them through composition (in a finite way) are all recursive functions. Church-Turing thesis: any function that can be computed by a human operator using pen and pencil can be computed by a TM. We assume the Thesis (Church-Turing Thesis) that every computable function, in the intuitive sense, is a recursive function and viceversa, as the thesis cannot be proven. In all these years since the formulation of the thesis, none has proposed a function that can be computed by a human operator and not by a Turing machine. Also any procedure to augment the capability of a Turing machine (for example by increasing number of tapes) did not success. The definitions we have seen can be modified if instead of saying that the machine halts after the string, we say the machine halts to the left of the strings, or if we initiate the computations on any cell of tape instead of stating them before the given strings. These differences are just conventions used and anyone of them will be valid. The position where we start the computations is only relevant in relationship to the strings not to the concrete enumeration used. The alphabet or the cardinal of the alphabet we use is not relevant as we can do the same (possible in more computation steps) with the vertical bar alphabet (one symbol). Definition 8.32: Recursive set, DEC(TM) Given A  N n . We say that A is a recursive set iff there is a TM M such that: for every vector (m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n )  N n , M put after the strings m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n it halts in a terminal configuration C = (q, E, z) such that : E(z)  a 0  (m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n )  A In this case we say that the TM decides set A . We call DEC(TM) to the set of all recursive sets. The TM always halts but depending whether it halts in an empty/non-empty cell, the vector does not / does belong to the set A. Definition 8.33: Recursively enumerable set, ENU(TM) Given A  N n . We say that A is a Recursively enumerable set iff there is a TM M such that for every vector (m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n )  N n , M put after strings m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n : M halts in a terminal configuration C = (q, E, z)   (m 1 , m 2 , ... , m n )  A E(z)  a 0 We also say in this case that the TM M semidecides A. We call ENU(TM) to the set of all recursively enumerable set. Note: DEC(TM)  ENU(TM) The TM halts and it does in an non-empty cell if the input vector belongs to the set A. If the vector does not belong to set A, the machine can go forever (not halt) or alternatively halt in an empty cell. Definition 8.34: Set generated by a TM Given A  N n . We say that A is a set generated by a TM iff A is a recursively enumerable set. Questions: Answer Yes or No and justify. Q1: A table used to define a TM can have an odd number of rows ? Recursive and recursively enumerable languages A recursively enumerable language is a formal language for which there exists a Turing machine (or other computable function) that will halt and accept when presented with any string in the language as input but may either halt and reject or loop forever when presented with a string not in the language. Contrast this to recursive languages, which require that the Turing machine halts in all cases. Type-3 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by FDA. Type-2 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by NDPA. Type-1 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by linear bounded TMs. Type-0 languages are recursively enumerable sets, they can only be semi-decided by TMs.

-- Initial (basic) Functions -- Combining functions: Operators -- Definition of the set of -Recursive Functions -- Examples -- Computability and further considerations UNIT 9 -Recursive Functions 9.1 Introduction We have seen in the previous unit the Turing Machine model to then formalize the concept of computable function. In this unit, we will analyze the concept of computable function in a constructive way, and the model will be implicitly defined. The idea behind "-recursive Functions" is quite simple: choose some very simple functions (initial functions) that we can consider to be trivially computable. Then define three operations over the functions (composition, primitive recursion and minimalization). We will see that a function is a -recursive function if it is an initial function or if it can be generated from the initial functions through the application (a finite number of times) of the three mentioned operations. In this way, the set of all -recursive functions (denoted as -REC) is the minimum set that contains the initial functions and that is closed for the operations of composition, primitive recursion and minimalization. Note: Do not get confused by the set of -recursive functions and the set of functions called primitive recursives (usually denoted as PRIM). The set PRIM is a proper subset of -REC, that is, all primitive recursive functions are -recursive functions but the converse is not true. Their definition is similar to -REC but the minimalization operator is not included, and thus all functions are total functions. The definition of the -REC set is a constructive definition. In fact is an inductive definition with a base hipothesis, three induction principles and one exclusion principle (to guarantee that the set is minimal). The set -REC can also be defined as the minimum class of functions that includes the initial functions and is closed for the operations: composition, primitive recursion and unbounded minimalization. According to the definition, given a recursive function, we know that whether is an initial function or is a function that can be generated by a finite sequence of the operations of composition, primitive recursion or unbounded minimization. The fact that the sequence is finite is what ensures that the function is representable, but we will se later on that not every representable function is a recursive function. Definition 9.20: Recursively decidable predicate, PRED(T-REC) The set PRED(T-REC) is defined as: PRED(T-REC) ={ P f | f T-REC } A given predicate P is recursively decidable iff P PRED(T-REC). Note: A predicate is recursively decidable if is a predicate associated to a total recursive function. PRED(T-REC), is thus the set of all recursively decidable predicate. Definition 9.21: Recursively enumerable predicate, PRED(-REC) The set PRED(-REC) is defined as: PRED(-REC) ={ P f | f -REC } A predicate P is recursively enumerable iff P PRED(-REC). Notes: A predicate is recusively enumerable if is the predicata associated to a recursively enumerable function. PRED(-REC) is then the set of all predicates recursively enumerable. It is verified that PRED(T-REC)  PRED(-REC) as T-REC  -REC . Definition 9.22: Set recursively decidable, DECI The set DECI is defined as: DECI ={ V P | P  PRED(T-REC) } We say that V  N k is a set recursively decidable iff V DECI . Notes: A set is recursively decidable if it is the set of True values of a recursively decidable predicate. DECI, is then the set of all sets that are recursively decidable. Definition 9.23: Recursively enumerable set , ENUM We define ENUM as: ENUM ={ V P | P  PRED(-REC) } V  N k i a Recursively enumerable set iff V ENUM. Notes: A set is recursively enumerable if it is the set of True values of a recusively enumerable predicate. ENUM is then the set of all sets that are recursively enumerable. DECI  ENUM as PRED(T-REC)  PRED(-REC) . Definition 9.24: Recursively generated set A set V is recursively generated iff V is recursively enumerable.
